narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score and middle-class family background, you've put real effort into your law school journey, and I appreciate how challenging it can be to receive this news. The admissions model evaluated your profile against thousands of past students' data to predict your chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 55.5% probability that you would fail—crossing the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction hinges on how your academic record stacks up, particularly your lower placement in Year 3 grades compared to peers, which stood out as the strongest factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure. Your LSAT score of 37, slightly above average, worked strongly in your favor, countering that risk by suggesting solid reasoning skills for the bar. Meanwhile, your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, a bit below the typical 3.21, also nudged things positively but not enough to offset the concerns. Your Year 1 decile ranking of 3 and family income bracket played smaller roles in tipping toward higher failure risk, while planning to attend full-time aligned with the vast majority of successful students. Overall, your profile shows strengths like that LSAT performance that match or exceed averages, but the dip in later high school performance set you apart from typical admits who maintain stronger consistent rankings. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's the model's data-driven read on bar passage patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further or strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program to better align with top performers. You might also explore part-time options or schools with holistic reviews that weigh your full story. Keep pushing; many students in similar spots have turned things around with targeted improvements. We're rooting for you to ace that bar on the first go.
